findings.title: scipy and portalocker are hard imports but absent from requirement.txt
findings.quote:
import primefac
import scipy
import math
findings.claim: gptq.py imports scipy at module load and resultutils.py imports portalocker at module load; both are imported transitively by the main entrypoint src/llama.py (which does `from gptq import *` and `from resultutils import *`), yet neither scipy nor portalocker appears in requirement.txt.
findings.concern: A fresh environment built from requirement.txt cannot even import src/llama.py (ImportError on scipy), so no reported result is reproducible without out-of-band dependency installation.
findings.resolution: Add scipy and portalocker (and confirm numpy/datasets/tqdm) to requirement.txt with the versions used for the paper.
findings.paper_ref: Code availability (NeurIPS checklist Q5: 'README file with a list of required packages')
findings.reason: Confirmed: gptq.py line 17 has `import scipy` and resultutils.py line 6 has `import portalocker`; neither appears in requirement.txt.

findings.title: No code computes Fig. 2 (per-block quantization-error accumulation ∆m)
findings.quote:
This experiment sets n = 10. Figure 2 shows an approximately exponential accumulation or
errors within the quantized layer, as well as an error growth that persists in the unquantized layers.
findings.claim: Figure 2 quantizes only the first 10 Transformer blocks, keeps the rest in FP, and plots the squared Frobenius norm ∆m (Eq. 2) of original-vs-partially-quantized block outputs; no script in src/ performs partial (first-n-blocks-only) quantization or computes a per-block output-difference norm (grep for the relevant logic returns nothing).
findings.concern: Figure 2 is the motivating empirical evidence for the paper's central premise (cross-layer error growth), yet the experiment that produces it is not in the repo and cannot be reproduced.
findings.resolution: Provide the script that quantizes the first n=10 blocks and evaluates ∆m per block (Eq. 2).
findings.reason: Paper text confirms the n=10 partial-quantization experiment (lines 249, 268) but the src/ directory contains only awq/, gptq.py, llama.py, qep_awq.py, and supporting utils — no script performing partial-block quantization or per-block Frobenius norm computation is present.

findings.title: No timing code for Table 3 quantization-runtime measurements
findings.quote:
Runtime
We examine the impact of computation time
required for the correction term. Table 3 shows the processing time of each layer-wise PTQ.
findings.claim: Table 3 reports wall-clock quantization times (GPTQ/AWQ/QEP+RTN at 7B/13B/70B), but the main quantization drivers src/llama.py and src/qep_awq.py contain no time.time()/perf_counter instrumentation (timing exists only in the unused src/zeroShot/models/gptq.py).
findings.concern: The runtime claims (e.g. 'QEP correction requires significantly less computation than the quantization process') are not reproducible from the code as the measurement harness is absent.
findings.resolution: Provide the timing harness used for Table 3, or state how the times were measured.
findings.reason: Original claim is correct; Sonnet's downgrade rested on a misread of line numbers. A grep of all of src/ for `time.time`/`tick` returns matches ONLY in src/zeroShot/models/gptq.py (lines 66, 131: `tick = time.time()` / `print('time %.2f' % (time.time() - tick))`) and src/zeroShot/models/fast_trueobs.py (lines 70, 162) — exactly the unused zeroShot tree the claim names. The main driver src/llama.py has `import time` at line 1 but NO `time.`/`tick` usage anywhere (its lines 66 and 131 are `model(batch[0].to(dev))` and the `run_weight_correct(...)` call, not timing); the import is dead. src/qep_awq.py has no timing at all. No script aggregates/reports the Table 3 runtimes across models/methods (GPTQ/AWQ/QEP+RTN at 7B/13B/70B): grep for perf_counter/elapsed/runtime/processing-time/wall-clock across src/ returns only an unrelated RuntimeError in evaluator.py. The Table-3 measurement harness is genuinely absent from the main quantization drivers.
findings.changed: reverted Sonnet's lowered->keep; confidence restored medium->high; severity stays low. Sonnet's reason (claiming llama.py lines 66/131 hold a time.time pair) was a line-number misattribution to src/zeroShot/models/gptq.py lines 66/131; the original claim that timing exists only in the unused zeroShot tree is verified correct.

findings.title: OmniQuant comparison (Table 11) has no code in the repo
findings.quote:
For completeness, we compare QEP-enhanced layerwise PTQ with block-wise OmniQuant [Shao et al.,
2023] on LLaMA-2-7B using WikiText-2 perplexity; lower values indicate better performance.
findings.claim: Appendix D.4 / Table 11 reports an OmniQuant baseline, but the repo contains no OmniQuant implementation or runner (grep for 'omni' in src/ returns nothing); only RTN/GPTQ/AWQ/QuIP are implemented.
findings.concern: The OmniQuant comparison row cannot be reproduced from this repo; the reader must trust the externally-obtained number.
findings.resolution: State the OmniQuant code/version used, or include a runner; otherwise mark Table 11's OmniQuant row as reproduced from an external implementation.
findings.reason: Paper text confirms the OmniQuant comparison in Appendix D.4 (line 2555) and grep of src/ returns nothing for 'omni', confirming no OmniQuant runner exists in the repo.

findings.title: README has no run commands or results table, contradicting checklist claim
findings.quote:
# Fujitsu Open Source Model Compression Software
- Release Plan
  - NeurIPS paper version release (December 2, 2025)
  - Official version release (March, 2026)
- Citation
findings.claim: The README contains only a release plan and a citation; it has no list of required packages, no example commands, and no results table, whereas the NeurIPS checklist (Q5) states the submission includes 'execution scripts and a README file with a list of required packages'.
findings.concern: Without exact reproduction commands or a results table, mapping CLI flags to specific paper table cells (bit-width, group size, α per model) is left to the reader to reverse-engineer.
findings.resolution: Add a README section with the exact commands that reproduce each main table/figure and the package list.
findings.paper_ref: NeurIPS checklist Q5 (Open access to data and code)
findings.reason: README.md lines 1-15 exactly match the quoted content — only a release plan and citation, with no run commands, no package list, and no results table.

findings.title: save_experiment_results IndexErrors for non-Llama models (e.g. Mistral-7B)
findings.quote:
pattern_llama = r'llama-(\d+)'
pattern_B = r'(\d+)b'
llama_match = re.findall(pattern_llama, args.model)
B_match = re.findall(pattern_B, args.model)
model_str = f"llama-{llama_match[0]}-{B_match[0]}b"
findings.claim: save_experiment_results parses the model name assuming it contains 'llama-<n>' and '<n>b'; for Mistral-7B (a model the paper evaluates) llama_match is empty, so llama_match[0] raises IndexError.
findings.concern: Running any Mistral-7B experiment with --save-result crashes at result-saving time; the metric is computed but cannot be persisted via this path.
findings.resolution: Make the model-name parsing robust to non-Llama models (Mistral, Llama-3), or guard the regex match.
findings.reason: resultutils.py lines 20-24 exactly match the quoted code; `llama_match[0]` on a Mistral model name would produce an empty list and raise IndexError as claimed.

findings.title: Model/layer-specific αl (α=0 for Llama-2-70B MLP) not implementable from CLI
findings.quote:
def run_weight_correct(
    self, layer, percdamp=.01, perccorr=.25
):
    W = layer.weight.data.clone()
    if isinstance(layer, nn.Conv2d):
        W = W.flatten(1)
    if isinstance(layer, transformers.Conv1D):
        W = W.t()
    W = W.float()
    H = self.H.clone()

    dead = torch.diag(H) == 0
    H[dead, dead] = 1
    W[:, dead] = 0

    damp = percdamp * torch.mean(torch.diag(H))
    diag = torch.arange(H.shape[0], device=H.device)
    H[diag, diag] += damp
    H = torch.linalg.cholesky(H)
    H = torch.cholesky_inverse(H)
    Hinv = H

    # 重み補正
    W += (W @ self.H_delta @ Hinv) * perccorr
findings.claim: The propagation strength α (=perccorr) is a single global scalar passed identically to every corrected layer; src/llama.py and src/qep_awq.py call run_weight_correct(..., perccorr=args.perccorr) for all attention/MLP-up/gate layers with no per-layer or per-model branch.
findings.concern: The paper sets αl = 1/2 for all layers EXCEPT MLP layers in Llama-2-70B which are set to αl = 0; with only a global --perccorr, a single run cannot apply α=0.5 to attention and α=0 to MLP simultaneously, so the exact Llama-2-70B configuration behind Table 1/2 is not reproducible from the CLI as shipped.
findings.resolution: Expose a per-layer / per-module α schedule (or a flag that zeroes correction for MLP layers), or document the exact command(s) used to obtain the Llama-2-70B numbers.
findings.paper_ref: Section 6 (Quantization): 'αl = 1/2 for all layers, except for the MLP layers in the Llama-2 70B model, for which we set αl = 0'
findings.reason: gptq.py lines 246-269 exactly match the quoted code; llama.py line 314 confirms a single global --perccorr argument; paper text line 481 confirms αl=0 for MLP in Llama-2 70B, which is not achievable via the CLI alone.

findings.title: Main tables report the best of 5 seeds per configuration
findings.quote:
The main text lists the best seed per configuration for
consistency with past work. This appendix confirms that the gains are not seed-specific but robust
findings.claim: Appendix D.3 states the main-text tables (Table 1/2) report the best seed per configuration; the code (src/llama.py --seed) runs a single seed per invocation and provides no max-over-seeds selection script, so the 'best seed' selection was done manually off-repo.
findings.concern: Reporting the best of 5 seeds (rather than the mean) optimistically biases the headline perplexity/accuracy numbers; the selection step is not in the repo and is only disclosed in the appendix.
findings.resolution: Report mean ± SEM over the 5 seeds in the main tables (as Fig. 3 does for QuIP), or clearly mark Table 1/2 values as best-of-5 and provide the selection script.
findings.paper_ref: Appendix D.3 (Stability of QuIP Results Across Random Seeds)
findings.reason: Paper text lines 2525-2526 exactly match the quoted passage; the disclosure is in the appendix only and no seed-selection script exists in the repo.

findings.title: mlp.down_proj is unconditionally excluded from QEP correction
findings.claim: In both drivers (llama.py and qep_awq.py) the QEP weight correction is skipped for mlp.down_proj for every model (the comment 'down_proj does not perform correction'), so down_proj always behaves as α=0 regardless of the --perccorr setting.
findings.concern: The paper presents QEP correction as applied across layers with a tunable αl; the unconditional exclusion of down_proj for all models is a fixed design choice the main text does not state, which slightly changes which weights are actually corrected.
findings.resolution: Document that down_proj is always excluded from correction, or make it configurable; clarify whether the reported gains include this exclusion.
findings.reason: llama.py lines 127-131 exactly match the quoted code; the `name != 'mlp.down_proj'` guard unconditionally excludes down_proj from correction for all models.
